narrative:
Today is hot as high temperatures approach 90. Smoke from wildfires in Canada create hazy skies at times today and tomorrow. We are dry today but isolated storms could develop Friday. The weekend is more humid, although not as warm, and afternoon and evening storms are scattered around.

2027 Blue Ridge Calendar

The 2027 Blue Ridge Calendar is here and being delivered to our retail outlets. Thanks for a record-breaking pre-publication sale! You can still avoid shipping costs with our pickup events beginning Thursday, July 2, in Boone. We'll take that to Banner Elk, West Jefferson, Spruce Pine, and Black Mountain next week; click here for details. You can see all the winning photography at our Photo Contest page . Check out volume discounts on the calendar and the "Mix and Match" sale on Drinkware.

Get the free RWC Mobile App...: Apple and Android

Hi: 87

Show map Boone Right Now 68.0°F Today's forecast: Mostly sunny but hazy skies at times; Patchy AM fog; On the hot side; Light NW wind. High 87° Station sponsor Forecast Current Graphics Archives Webcams Heat and Haze Last Updated Thu July 16 at 10:20AM by Wayne Presnell Today is hot as high temperatures approach 90. Smoke from wildfires in Canada create hazy skies at times today and tomorrow. We are dry today but isolated storms could develop Friday. The weekend is more humid, although not as warm, and afternoon and evening storms are scattered around. 2027 Blue Ridge Calendar The 2027 Blue Ridge Calendar is here and being delivered to our retail outlets. Thanks for a record-breaking pre-publication sale! You can still avoid shipping costs with our pickup events beginning Thursday, July 2, in Boone. We'll take that to Banner Elk, West Jefferson, Spruce Pine, and Black Mountain next week; click here for details. You can see all the winning photography at our Photo Contest page . Check out volume discounts on the calendar and the "Mix and Match" sale on Drinkware. Subscribe Wednesday Extended forecast is only available for RaysWeather.com supporters. Subscribe Forecast Discussion High pressure aloft and at the surface holds on for one more day. Today is hot and dry, approaching 90 in many of lower elevation locations. Smoke from Canadian wildfires make our skies hazy at times today and tomorrow. The density of the smoke is nothing like that in the Great Lakes and Northeast though. A cold front will move in slowly and stall just to the north Friday and Saturday. This increases the humidity and provides the energy for isolated afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms Friday. The coverage increases to scattered Saturday. Afternoon temperatures are a little lower due to more clouds. Another cold front moves in from the north Sunday keeping scattered afternoon and evening storms in the forecast. This front makes a little further south before washing out Monday. Drier air works in Monday decreasing the coverage of showers and thunderstorms. A more significant cold front approaches from the northwest Tuesday and should pass through Wednesday. This front continues the risk for afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms but also brings hopes for cooler and drier air for the middle of next week. Detailed forecast discussion and analysis is only available to RaysWeather supporters. Subscribe Log in Wayne Presnell Meteorologist PHOTO OF THE DAY CLICK TO ENLARGE Watauga Lake sunset. Submitted by Magnolis. All Photos Submit Photo Latest Insights Monday Message for July 13, 2026 This week's Monday Message highlights the badly-needed wet weather over the weekend, to be followed by drier and hotter weather for the remainder of the workweek. Thanks to our subscribers for your support! Jul 13, 2026 June 2026 Recap Plain Old Summer We kicked off the summer of 2026 with a fairly typical month of June. Temperatures were fairly steady, and largely near or just above normal. Although scattered showers and thunderstorms were a regularity, we did not have many days where storms were especially widespread, and thus the vast majority of the region wound up below normal once again on monthly precipitation. We'll get more into that later on. The month started out rather pleasantly, with most stations notching their coolest low temperature on the morning of the 3rd. Only a handful of stations failed to drop below 50 degrees that morning, which is a fairly impressive chill for the time of year. In southern Ashe County, Todd managed to drop to 38.5F before rallying to nearly 74 degrees for the high temperature, which is the sort of day we expect to see in late spring. Nearby in Boone, the morning low of 39F got within 3 degrees of the record low for the date (36F, set in 1956). That 39F is around 13 degrees below normal for early June. Down in Black Mountain, 23 of 30 days in June reached at least 80 degrees, and 4 made it to 90F. Two mornings managed to stay at 70F or more, which is a fairly impressive level of warmth for a sunrise in the valley at 2,300 feet. Like many other stations, a decent chunk of the month's rainfall came in a few days towards the end of the month, as 0.92" fell in 3 days - close to half of the monthly total there. Surry Co. Slammers As you will see later on in the precipitation section of this recap, only one of our major stations exceeded their monthly average rainfall: the Surry County Airport near Mount Airy. In fact, two individual days, the 10th and the 18th, produced 4.52 inches of rain, which would have been the most of any of the stations included in the precipitation table even without adding in the other 28 days of the month! Summer is a time of "winners and losers" when it comes to rainfall, which is illustrated even further by the maps below (courtesy of NOAA). The Surry Co. Airport picked up 2.45" on the 10th in the image on the left, while just a couple miles up the road closer to the town of Mt. Airy, less than half of that fell. Not to mention that southern and western Surry County only recorded 0.01"! A more widespread rainfall occurred on the 18th, with other stations nearby exceeding 2 inches along with the airport site. Once you add in the other 28 days of June, the Surry Co. Airport recorded 6.23 inches of rain, just over 125 percent of a normal June. The next highest percentage in our recap? 81.0 percent on Beech Mountain. This time around, Mount Airy was the winner for hit-or-miss thunderstorms. Temperatures June did not bring much that fell very far outside the envelope of "ordinary" this year. The aforementioned chill of the morning of June 3rd brought some of the greatest departures from normal for the month, but outside of that, things stayed within 10 or so degrees of normal. Now you may think upon reading that statement: "Well, of course they stayed close to normal. That's why it is called normal!" A fair reaction, to be certain. The deep summer months of June, July, August, and even September to some degree, do not bring quite as many moments with temperatures departing the averages as fall, winter, and spring do. If you go and read one of our recaps from the any of those months, you will see that the averages are relatively far from the experienced temperatures with some regularity. In summer, cold fronts are weak and transient, bringing only minor "cooldowns." It is easier to get far away from average with excessive heat, as we did to start July. To illustrate this, take a look at the data from the Hickory Regional Airport in June. Only one out of thirty high temperatures and two out of thirty low temperatures in June were 10 or more degrees above or below normal, combining for 3/60 highs & lows. The 57 other highs/lows were within 10 degrees of average, and many were within 4 to 6 degrees. Back in April, 25 out of 60 highs or lows were 10 or more degrees away from average! The table and map below help illustrate the point further. Things just did not get very far from what you expect June to be, and they rarely ever do. The largest departures from normal lie with the low temperatures in the High Country being a decent bit above normal.

Precipitation As was discussed above, summer is the time of winners and losers when it comes to rainfall. Most were losers this time around. Burnsville, Jefferson, Lenoir, Mroganton, and North Wilkesboro all fell shy of 2 inches of rain for the month, while Beech Mountain recieved 4 inches (on the dot!) and Mt. Airy's good luck pushed them past 6 inches. The "biggest loser" for the month was Morganton, where only 27.1% of average June rainfall occurred. We had many days where storms favored the mountains (as they often do, thanks to the terrain creating better opoortunities to spark storms), and Morganton failed to cash in on the scattered chances in the Foothills. The map below displays the luck of Mount Airy compared to the rest of Surry County... northwestern portions of the county recieved less than 50% of average precipitation for the month! Our only other contender for above average precipitation was Wolf Laurel, where over 5 inches of rain fell. Again, all thanks to their good luck on seeing afternoon storms up on the ridgeline.
